Article Title: Niyati Oxford Law Graduate: ‘My goal was not immigration’: Oxford law graduate on why she’s returning to India from UK
Article Text:
Oxford law graduate picks India over UK career





Why returning to India felt right

“I don’t know what the future holds”

What is “regret minimisation”?







An Indian student, Niyati, has shared why she plans to return home just two months after graduating from University of Oxford with a law degree, despite having the option to build a career in the United Kingdom.In a video posted on Instagram, she explained that her decision is not driven by uncertainty or lack of opportunity abroad, but by a conscious effort to avoid future regret.While she described her time in the UK as fulfilling and said she may return someday, she clarified that settling there was never her end goal.“My primary aim was always to get a quality education, not to immigrate,” she said, highlighting that her journey abroad had a clear purpose from the start.Although staying back and pursuing a legal career in the UK remained a viable path, Niyati said she felt a stronger pull toward India—particularly due to her commitment to Pratisandhi, an initiative she is deeply invested in.She explained that not returning home and giving the initiative a fair chance to grow would likely lead to long-term regret. For her, the opportunity to contribute meaningfully in India outweighed the appeal of an established career track overseas.Her decision reflects a growing sentiment among some Indian students abroad—prioritising impact and personal alignment over conventional definitions of success.Niyati acknowledged that her decision comes with uncertainty. She admitted she cannot predict whether returning to India will ultimately prove to be the “right” choice.However, she emphasised that her guiding principle remains clear—making decisions she is less likely to regret in the long run.At the core of Niyati’s decision is the idea of regret minimisation—a framework popularised by Jeff Bezos when he decided to leave a stable job to start Amazon.The concept is simple but powerful: imagine yourself in the future (often at an older age) and ask whether you would regret not taking a particular step. Instead of being guided by short-term fears or uncertainties, the focus shifts to long-term fulfilment.For many, this approach encourages taking calculated risks and making choices aligned with deeper values rather than immediate comfort.
Article Summary: Indian student, Niyati, has shared why she plans to return home just two months after graduating from University of Oxford with a law degree. She explained that her decision is not driven by uncertainty or lack of opportunity abroad, but by a conscious effort to avoid future regret. For her, the opportunity to contribute meaningfully in India outweighed the appeal of an established career track overseas.

Article Title: King Charles 'delighted' as Princess Eugenie announces third pregnancy with Jack Brooksbank
Article Text:
FILE - Britain's Britain's Princess Eugenie and Jack Brooksbank attend the Together At Christmas carol service at Westminster Abbey, in London, Thursday, Dec. 15, 2022. (Henry Nicholls/Pool Photo via AP, File)







Princess Eugenie is expecting her third child with husband Jack Brooksbank this summer. The 36-year-old princess announced the pregnancy on Instagram on Monday morning posting a photograph of her two sons August and Ernest holding an ultrasound image alongside the caption "Baby Brooksbank due 2026. "Buckingham Palace confirmed the news in a formal statement saying the King had been informed and was delighted. The palace said the couple's two boys, August aged five and Ernest aged two, were very excited to welcome a new sibling into the family, Sky News reported.The new baby will be born 15th in line to the throne and will not hold an HRH title. As the niece of King Charles III, Princess Eugenie’s growing family continues to draw public interest. The arrival will also move the Duke of Edinburgh to 16th place in the line of succession. The child will be the 15th great-grandchild of the late Queen Elizabeth II and the third to be born since her death in 2022. The baby will also be the fifth grandchild of the former Duke and Duchess of York, following the birth of Princess Beatrice’s daughter, Athena Mapelli Mozzi, in January last year.The palace announcement comes at a complicated moment for the family.Eugenie's father Andrew was arrested in February on suspicion of misconduct in public office over allegations that he shared sensitive information with convicted paedophile financier Jeffrey Epstein. Andrew denies any wrongdoing. In November last year the King stripped Andrew of his prince title, his HRH style and his dukedom because of his association with Epstein.Buckingham Palace's decision to issue a warm formal statement welcoming the pregnancy signals continued royal support for Eugenie personally regardless of the circumstances surrounding Andrew. Eugenie married Jack Brooksbank at Windsor Castle in 2018 and the couple have built a family largely away from the frontline of royal public duties.
Article Summary: Princess Eugenie is expecting her third child with husband Jack Brooksbank. The new baby will be born 15th in line to the throne and will not hold an HRH title. The arrival will move the Duke of Edinburgh to 16th place in the line of succession.

Article Title: ‘Hot for Them Too’: British Man Praised Online for Rescuing Dehydrated Bird Amid Delhi’s Heatwave
Article Text:
Nick Booker praised for rescuing dehydrated bird amid Delhi heatwave.







A British national has been widely praised online after rescuing a dehydrated bird in Delhi’s ongoing heatwave, drawing attention to the impact of extreme temperatures on urban wildlife.Nick Booker shared a video showing how he came across a distressed bird struggling in the heat and stepped in to help.In the footage, recorded during what he described as one of the hottest days of the year, temperatures in Delhi had reached around 41 degrees Celsius.Booker said he found the bird — identified as a Black Kite — unable to move due to dehydration.“If it is hot for us - it is hot for them! Many birds are really suffering in the heatwave,” he wrote.He is seen gently moving the bird into the shade of a tree before offering it water, which it quickly drank.“On the hottest day of the year, it's 41 degrees here in Delhi, we're in the middle of the heatwave,” he said in the video.He also contacted wildlife rescue organisation Wildlife SOS, which responded promptly.According to Booker, the team arrived within about 20 minutes to assist the bird.He thanked members of the organisation for their quick response, saying: “Huge thanks to @wildlifesos, especially Sanoj Chaurasiya for taking my call and Pratap Singh for coming to the rescue!”The video has been widely appreciated online, with many users praising the quick response and highlighting the importance of helping animals during extreme weather conditions.The incident also pointed to a wider concern during the heatwave. Booker noted that rescue teams were dealing with a sharp rise in distress calls involving birds.He said around 15 birds had been rescued on that day alone, compared to just two or three on a typical day.Urging people to take simple steps to help, he added: “Please do put out water on your balconies, on your terraces, in front of your homes because the birds really need to drink.”The video highlights the growing impact of rising temperatures on wildlife, as heatwave conditions continue across parts of northern India.
Article Summary: Nick Booker shared a video showing how he came across a distressed bird struggling in the heat and stepped in to help. In the footage, recorded during what he described as one of the hottest days of the year, temperatures in Delhi had reached around 41 degrees Celsius. The video has been widely appreciated online, with many users praising the quick response.

Article Title: Indian-origin doctor shares experience of UK driving test, says system is ‘strict but necessary’
Article Text:
Sunita Sayammagaru







An Indian-origin doctor has shared her experience of obtaining a driving licence in the UK, describing the process as significantly more rigorous compared to India.Sunita Sayammagaru wrote in a post on X that while obtaining a driving licence in India felt relatively easy, the UK system required a much higher level of skill and awareness.She said she already held an Indian licence when she moved to the UK in 2007, but soon realised her driving standards were not up to the mark.“I realised how pathetic my driving was,” she wrote, adding that she had not been taught a large part of what is expected from drivers in the UK.According to her post, the UK licensing process involves both a theory test and a practical exam, where candidates are closely assessed on multiple aspects of driving.She described how examiners check mirror usage, lane discipline, indicators, parking skills, speed control and the ability to navigate complex roundabouts.During her first attempt, she failed the practical test.She said the examiner pointed out a major fault — failing to complete a full mirror and shoulder check before moving out at a roundabout — despite otherwise acceptable driving.“He said the rest of my driving was OK and that he was strict with me, but it was for my good,” she wrote.She reapplied for a test two weeks later and was assessed again by the same examiner, which she said made her nervous.However, on her second attempt, she passed.Sayammagaru also noted that several Indian drivers she knew had failed the UK test multiple times despite years of driving experience back home.“It’s that strict,” she said.Despite the difficulty, she argued that such standards are necessary.“Driving licence tests should be strict. This ensures safety of self and others,” she wrote.Her post has since resonated with many users online, with several agreeing that stricter testing can lead to safer roads.
Article Summary: Sunita Sayammagaru said obtaining a driving licence in India felt relatively easy. But the UK system required a much higher level of skill and awareness, she said. She said she already held an Indian licence when she moved to the UK in 2007.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Princess Diana: “I don’t go by a rule book … I lead from the heart and not the head.”
Article Text:
Princess Diana

Watch Princess Diana’s Biggest Regret: BBC Interview ‘HURT MY BOYS,’ Insider Reveal | WATCH Princess Diana’s Biggest Regret: BBC Interview ‘HURT MY BOYS,’ Insider Reveal | WATCH

Quote of the day by Princess Diana

“I don’t go by a rule book … I lead from the heart and not the head.”

What the quote by Princess Diana means in everyday language

A closer look at leadership beyond rules

The importance of empathy in human interaction

Why flexibility matters in real-life situations

Emotional intelligence in action

Public life shaped by human connection

The balance between heart and head

Relevance in modern workplaces

How the quote by Princess Diana applies in personal life

Famous quotes by Princess Diana

“I’d like to be queen of people’s hearts, in people’s hearts.”

"I want my boys to have an understanding of people's emotions, their insecurities, people's distress, and their hopes and dreams."

"Every one of us needs to show how much we care for each other and, in the process, care for ourselves."

"So many people supported me through my public life, and I will never forget them."

"I'm aware that people I have loved and have died and are in the spirit world looking after me."

“Carry out a random act of kindness, with no expectation of reward, safe in the knowledge that one day someone might do the same for you.”

A lasting message about human values

Final takeaway from the quote by Princess Diana







People often remember public figures not just for what they did, but also for how they made them feel. Princess Diana said, “I don’t go by a rule book … I lead from the heart and not the head” is a way of thinking that goes beyond what is expected of you in your job. Diana, Princess of Wales, was known for being able to connect with people on a personal level while she was in the public eye. Her visits to hospitals, meetings with children, and interactions with communities were all unique because they were based on empathy and understanding rather than strict adherence to tradition.This quote brings up a simple but strong idea. It implies that not every decision should be solely determined by logic or established regulations. In a lot of cases, compassion and human emotions can be just as important. The words still mean something because they talk about a way of leading and acting that people can see in their daily lives. This quote helps us understand how being aware of our feelings can change how we act and how we relate to other people.There are two parts to the quote. The first part, "I don't go by a rule book," means that you should be open to different ways of doing things.It shows that not every action is based on rules or instructions that have already been set, allowing creativity, flexibility, innovation, and independent thinking to guide decisions in changing situations. The second part, "I lead from the heart and not the head," means that feelings, empathy, and personal understanding play a role in making decisions.These ideas together describe a way of acting that puts a lot of value on emotional connection. Diana, Princess of Wales, says that leadership or behaviour isn't just about following rules or logic. It also means knowing people and smartly meeting their needs.People often think of structure, planning, and discipline when they think of leadership. Rules help keep things clear and orderly in a lot of businesses and organisations. The quote, on the other hand, shows a different side of leadership. It implies that human comprehension can also influence choices.Princess Diana's way of leading showed that leaders can listen to people, understand their problems, and respond with care. This doesn't replace structure; it just adds to it. It shows that being a leader is not just about having power; it's also about making connections.Empathy is the ability to understand and share how other people feel. It is an important part of how people get along with each other at work and in their personal lives. The quote talks about leading with the heart, which is the same thing as having empathy.Empathy makes it easier to talk to people in everyday life. It lets people respond in a way that takes into account how others feel. It can help people work together and be a team at work. It helps families keep strong ties with each other.The quote's message is that being aware of your feelings is a part of making decisions. It can be a big part of it, though.Not following a "rule book" doesn't mean completely ignoring rules. It shows that we need to be able to change. Real-life situations are often complicated and don't always fit into neat boxes.For instance, helping someone in need may mean thinking quickly and understanding rather than following the rules exactly. In the same way, talking to different people may call for different methods.This flexibility helps people deal with changing situations better. The quote emphasises this capacity for adaptation as a significant facet of behaviour.Emotional intelligence is the ability to notice, understand, and deal with feelings. It also includes being able to respond to how other people feel. The quote reflects this concept in a simple way.Leading from the heart can be thought of as using emotional intelligence to make choices. Being aware of your feelings and using that awareness to make decisions is what it means. You can see this in areas like healthcare, education, and social work, where it's important to understand people.Diana, Princess of Wales, became well-known for her work with charities and her public appearances. She often interacted directly with people, which helped bring attention to important social issues.She went to hospitals, helped with charity projects, and talked to people who were having problems. These actions were often based on empathy and concern, not just what was expected of them.The quote shows this part of her public life. It shows how personal connections can change how people think and know about things.The quote talks about balance, but it also talks about leading from the heart. Making decisions often involves both feelings and logic.Logic aids in planning, analysis, and organisation. Feeling things helps you understand people and respond with care. In a lot of cases, both sides work together.The quote makes it clear that you shouldn't depend on just one method. It implies that emotional comprehension can be pivotal in conjunction with organised reasoning.Communication and working together are important in today's workplaces. Employees work with other people, talk to clients, and do different jobs. In these kinds of situations, getting to know other people can help things go better.Flexibility can help solve problems, and empathy can help people work together better. The quote's message applies to these places, where working with other people is a big part of the job.The ideas in the quote can be used in more than just public roles or work settings. You can also use them in your daily life. Understanding and compassion are very important in families, friendships, and communities.Everyday interactions include listening to others, responding with care, and adjusting to different situations. The quote makes these actions stand out as important parts of behaviour.People keep sharing the quote because it shows what people value. It emphasises empathy, comprehension, and connection. These values are still important in different times and places.The quote makes it easy to understand and use because it presents a simple idea in clear language. It demonstrates that actions motivated by compassion can yield significant effects.Diana, Princess of Wales, said something that clearly shows how people act and how to lead. It shows how important it is to be empathetic, flexible, and emotionally aware when making decisions.The message is still true in many areas of life, including work and personal relationships. It shows that rules and logic are important, but so is human connection when it comes to actions and results.
Article Summary: Princess Diana said, “I don’t go by a rule book … I lead from the heart and not the head” is a way of thinking that goes beyond what is expected of you in your job. Diana, Princess of Wales, was known for being able to connect with people on a personal level while she was in the public eye.

Article Title: 2026 London Marathon raises £87.5m for charity, sets new fundraising world record
Article Text:
London Marathon 2026







The 2026 London Marathon has raised a record £87.5 million for charity, making it the biggest one-day fundraising event of its kind so far this year.Organisers said the total has already surpassed last year’s £87.3 million, with the final figure expected to exceed £90 million when confirmed later this year, the BBC reported.The race, held in London last Sunday, also saw standout performances on the course. Kenya’s Sabastian Sawe made history by becoming the first athlete to complete an official marathon in under two hours, finishing in 1 hour, 59 minutes and 30 seconds.In the women’s race, Ethiopia’s Tigst Assefa set a new women-only world record with a time of 2 hours, 15 minutes and 41 seconds.The event also set a participation milestone, with 59,830 runners crossing the finish line — the highest number recorded in a marathon.Hugh Brasher, chief executive of London Marathon Events, described the day as exceptional.“It was an extraordinary day of world records, from the elite men and women running such extraordinary times, to the participation numbers, and now in fundraising,” he said.He added that the scale of support from runners and donors had pushed the event to new heights.“It is fantastic to know we have already surpassed our £87.3m world record from 2025,” he said. “Huge thanks to everyone who fundraised, and to everyone who donated to such a wide range of charities.”The final fundraising total will be officially confirmed in September.
Article Summary: The 2026 London Marathon has raised a record £87.5 million for charity. It is the biggest one-day fundraising event of its kind so far this year. Organisers said the total has already surpassed last year’s £ 87.3 million. The final fundraising total will be officially confirmed in September.

Article Title: She said four words, it saved her father's life — and are now a legal right in England
Article Text:
UK health reports







When Karen Osenton walked into her father's ward at the John Radcliffe Hospital in Oxford last summer, she saw a man deteriorating rapidly. David Osenton, a retired engineer in his early 70s who had been fit and healthy before his admission, was by then thin, jaundiced and barely able to lift his head. He had spent six days in the cardiac ward, and his condition appeared to worsen each day.Karen kept noticing a poster above his bed explaining a patient’s right to request a second opinion under 'Martha’s Rule'. On the sixth day, she decided to act.According to The Guardian, Karen told staff she was invoking Martha's Rule and demanded an urgent review by a different clinical team — a move now credited with saving her father’s life. David’s condition had been deteriorating due to a combination of delays and missed warning signs. The second opinion changed the course of his treatment.Introduced in England in 2024, Martha’s Rule gives patients and their families the right to request an immediate review from an independent clinical team if they believe a patient’s condition is worsening and concerns are not being addressed. The rule is named after Martha Mills, a 13-year-old who died in 2021 after doctors at King’s College Hospital in London failed to escalate her care to intensive care in time.A coroner later concluded she would likely have survived if that escalation had happened. Her parents subsequently campaigned to turn that failure into a formal safeguard.The impact has been significant. Figures cited by The Guardian suggest the rule may have contributed to saving more than 500 lives since its introduction, reflecting cases where a second review led to changes in care.David Osenton's case illustrates why the rule matters. He was in a bed close to the nurses’ station and visibly unwell, yet his deterioration was not acted upon in time. It was his daughter who recognised the danger — and used the mechanism available to her.Martha’s Rule marks a shift in the balance between patients, families and the healthcare system. It recognises that those closest to a patient often spot warning signs first, and gives them a formal pathway to escalate concerns. Before this, families could only raise issues informally and hope they were taken seriously. Now, there is a clear right and a process to enforce it.David Osenton survived.
Article Summary: Martha’s Rule gives patients the right to request an immediate review from an independent clinical team if they believe a patient's condition is worsening. The rule is named after Martha Mills, a 13-year-old who died in 2021 after doctors failed to escalate her care to intensive care in time.

Article Title: Banksy artwork appears overnight in London, Mystery statue sparks debate
Article Text:
Members of the public look at a statue of a man holding a flag covering his face, and signed 'Banksy', which has appeared in Waterloo Place in London, Thursday, April 30, 2026. (AP Photo/Kin Cheung)







Banksy appears to have unveiled a new artwork in central London overnight. A large resin statue showed up on a plinth in Waterloo Place early Wednesday, close to statues of Florence Nightingale and Edward VII and near several major government buildings and embassies. There was no public notice or permission and by morning the piece was already drawing attention.According to the BBC, the statue shows a suited man stepping forward off a plinth with a flag covering his face. He looks confident, but he cannot see where he is going. The message is direct and appears intended to be taken that way.Banksy posted a video to Instagram on Thursday showing glimpses of how the installation was carried out. The footage shows yellow traffic cones marking off the area, a large vehicle pulling up, lorry-load stabilisers being deployed and a figure in an orange hi-viz vest directing the operation. The statue was lifted into position using a hook and the entire operation appeared to take only a matter of minutes.James Peak, creator of the BBC podcast series The Banksy Story, told the BBC the team behind the installation operates at a level most people would not expect from a street art crew.He described them as the sort of people who could set up a Metallica concert in 24 hours: organised, professional and clearly experienced at working quickly and quietly in urban environments. Peak said they had almost certainly surveyed the location in advance and timed the operation for the quietest possible window in the middle of the night.Westminster City Council confirmed to the BBC it had not granted permission for the installation because it received no advance notice. Despite that the council's response was notably warm. A spokesperson said the council was excited to see Banksy's latest sculpture in Westminster and described it as a striking addition to the city's public art scene. Safety barriers have since been erected around the statue and the council said it will remain accessible for the public to view while its options are considered.James Peak described the work as capturing a very precise moment in time in a way that most statues never manage. He said the image of a flag-blinded leader about to step off a precipice was a sharp commentary on what he called strong leadership and martial sentiment and suggested Banksy was questioning whether that kind of posturing leads anywhere useful at all.The location adds a further layer of meaning. Waterloo Place sits at the centre of British institutional power. It is surrounded by embassies, private members' clubs and the architecture of government. Placing a statue of a blindfolded authority figure in that specific setting is a deliberate choice rather than an accident of logistics.The statue is large enough that theft is considered unlikely though Banksy's work has been stolen before. In 2004 an earlier piece called The Drinker was removed from Shaftesbury Avenue and was not recovered for a decade. This new work will remain in place for now and London appears to be enjoying it.
Article Summary: Banksy appears to have unveiled a new artwork in central London overnight. A large resin statue showed up on a plinth in Waterloo Place early Wednesday. There was no public notice or permission and by morning the piece was already drawing attention. The statue shows a suited man stepping forward with a flag covering his face. He looks confident, but he cannot see where he is going.
